title: Window-cut page apertures
subtitle: Cutting openings that let the page beneath become part of the design
excerpt: Rectangular or shaped apertures cut through a single journal page reveal the page beneath as a deliberate design element. This technique covers planning, cutting through one layer only, and reinforcing the window edges.
sourceNotes: Window-cut page design is a contemporary journalling craft technique with no pre-1928 documented source. The geometry draws on paper engineering and bookbinding principles; the single-layer cutting method derives from the same controlled knife-work used in papercutting and matting.

glossaryTerms.definition: The first cut made at a corner of a rectangular aperture, inserted by plunging the knife point into the paper at 90 degrees before drawing the blade along the edge line.
An opening cut entirely through a sheet of paper or card so that whatever lies beneath is visible through the hole.
A cut that runs a fraction past the intended corner to ensure the waste piece releases cleanly, avoiding a torn tab of paper at the corner.

content.text: A window cut through one page of a journal turns the page below into part of the composition above. The colour, texture, or image on the lower page shows through the opening as if it were an inset panel. The technique is straightforward once you solve the main problem: cutting through exactly one layer without disturbing the pages beneath.

items.name: Cartridge paper, 130 gsm
items.qty: Cut to your journal page size, for the top page with the window
items.substitutions: Any standard journal page weight. Thinner papers (below 100 gsm) can tear at the corners under the knife; heavier papers (above 160 gsm) give better results.

items.name: Cartridge paper, 220 gsm
items.qty: Cut to the same size, for the backing page behind the window
items.substitutions: Coloured paper, printed paper, or marbled paper work well as the visible backing.

items.name: Washi tape
items.substitutions: Optional; used to reinforce thin window edges.

attrs.body: Sharp blades are safer than blunt blades; blunt blades skid. Change the blade at the first sign of dulling. Always cut on a self-healing cutting mat. Always cut against a metal ruler held firmly, fingers at least two finger-widths back from the blade's path, never in front of it.

content.text: Separate the page. Remove the top page from the journal and lay it on the cutting mat. Cutting single loose pages avoids the problem of the blade catching the pages underneath. If the page cannot be removed, slide a piece of thick card (2 mm greyboard works well) beneath the page you are cutting before starting.
in pencil. Mark the four corners of the rectangle lightly. Leave at least 15 mm of paper on all sides of the window; a narrower margin tears under the knife.
Cut the four sides one at a time. Place the metal ruler on the waste side of the marked line (not on the piece you want to keep). Plunge the knife at one corner; this is the
. Draw the blade along the ruler to the opposite corner, applying steady downward pressure. Lift the ruler, rotate the paper 90 degrees, and repeat for each side.

at each corner: let the blade travel about 1 mm past the corner point before lifting. This ensures the waste piece releases without leaving a tab of uncut paper at the corner. A clean interior angle is the difference between a window that looks precise and one that looks ragged.
Lift the waste piece out. If a corner has not cut through, resist the urge to tear it; re-run the blade along that edge. The paper will separate cleanly on the second pass.
Check the edges. Run a finger along the inside of the cut. Any rough spots can be tidied by holding the ruler against the edge and making one more light pass with the knife at a shallower angle.

Reinforce thin edges if needed. A narrow strip of washi tape applied to the back of the window aperture along each interior edge adds strength without adding visible bulk. This is worthwhile on papers below 100 gsm or where the window margin is less than 20 mm.

Position the backing page. Lay the cut page on top of the backing paper. Move the backing sheet around until the section visible through the window is what you want: a colour patch, a painted wash, a printed text, a photograph. Attach the two sheets together at their outer edges using a thin line of glue stick, or bind them into the journal as a pair.

items.symptom: The waste piece tears at the corners rather than releasing cleanly
items.cause: The corner cuts did not fully meet, leaving a connected tab of fibre.
items.fix: Place the ruler along the uncut edge and run the knife through again. An overcut of 1-2 mm past the corner point prevents this on future cuts.

items.symptom: The blade wanders off the ruler line mid-cut
items.cause: The blade is beginning to dull, or the ruler is shifting because it is not held firmly enough.
items.fix: Change the blade. Grip the ruler with your palm flat on it, not just your fingertips.

items.symptom: The top page cut through to the page below
items.cause: The knife angle was too steep, or the mat beneath was not present.
items.fix: For future cuts: always slide a piece of thick card (2 mm greyboard) between the page being cut and the pages below. Use a single, even pass rather than multiple light ones.

items.symptom: The cut edges are slightly rough and fibrous
items.cause: A dull blade dragged rather than cut the fibres.
items.fix: A fresh blade is the only reliable fix. Light sanding with 400-grit sandpaper, held flat against the edge, can smooth minor roughness after the fact.

content.text: Stacking two windows on consecutive pages creates depth: the page beneath the immediate backing shows through a smaller second opening, giving a layered, receding effect. Cut the second window smaller than the first and offset it slightly so both reveal different areas of the third page.

Non-rectangular apertures; circles, ovals, irregular shapes; use the same single-layer approach but are cut freehand without a ruler. Hold the knife close to vertical and cut in one continuous motion, rotating the paper rather than the wrist.
